flashcards.front: What does the ls command do in Unix/Linux?
flashcards.back: The ls command lists the contents of a directory, showing files and subdirectories. It is one of the most frequently used commands for directory navigation.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of ls -l option?
flashcards.back: ls -l displays files in long listing format showing details like permissions, owner, group, size, and modification time.
ls -a shows all files including hidden files (those starting with a dot .) which are normally not displayed.

flashcards.front: What is the ls -R command used for?
flashcards.back: ls -R lists subdirectories recursively, displaying the contents of all nested directories.
ls -i displays the inode number of each file before its name in the output.
ls -h displays file sizes in human-readable format (K, M, G) instead of raw bytes.

flashcards.front: What is the function of ls -t option?
flashcards.back: ls -t sorts files by modification time, showing the most recently modified files first.
ls -S sorts files by size, displaying the largest files first.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of ls -r option?
flashcards.back: ls -r reverses the order of sorting, displaying files in reverse order (alphabetically or by time/size).
ls -1 displays one file per line, useful for scripting and when output needs to be parsed.
ls -d lists directories themselves rather than their contents. When used with -l, shows directory details.

flashcards.front: What is the function of ls -F option?
flashcards.back: ls -F adds indicators to file names: / for directories, * for executable files, @ for symbolic links, and = for sockets.
ls --color colorizes the output based on file type: blue for directories, green for executables, etc.
ls -n is similar to -l but displays numeric UIDs and GIDs instead of owner/group names.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of combining ls -la?
flashcards.back: ls -la combines -l (long format) and -a (show hidden files), displaying all files with detailed information.
